one_line_ask: Digitize and restore one 1910 magician-troupe postcard — clarify and lighten the dark sepia front photo and sharpen the contrast of the handwritten back so the description is legible — and deliver each side as its own clean, deskewed, print-and-social-ready file.
brief: A family-history client is digitizing ~24 antique postcard photographs from 1910 documenting a famous stage magician and his traveling company touring Asia and abroad; the client's grandmother and aunt performed in the troupe and wrote a description of each scene on the BACK of the postcards. The client needs every postcard delivered as two separate restored files — the photographic FRONT as one picture and the handwritten BACK as another — that are clean enough to be pasted into a printed memorial book and posted to social media. Many of the front photos are dark, low-contrast, and tilted from flatbed scanning; the handwriting on the backs is faint cursive pencil/ink that is hard to read. This benchmark uses ONE representative postcard as the worked end-to-end example. The client supplies two raw flatbed scans: (1) the postcard front (a dark, slightly rotated sepia-toned albumen-style photograph of the magician and company on a theatre stage, with scanner-bed border) and (2) the postcard back (faded cursive handwriting plus a postmark and divided-back postcard printing, also slightly skewed). Required restoration pipeline, per side: ingest both raw scans into the workspace; auto-s
